risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "4,043 were determined to be eligible and randomized to one of the three trial arms" Comment: The article states that households were randomized, and it is described as a community-randomized controlled trial, which suggests allocation was intended to be random. However, no specific sequence-generation method is reported (e.g., computer-generated random numbers, random blocks, stratification, coin toss), so the adequacy of the random sequence cannot be confirmed.

_evidence.method_quote: “4,043 were determined to be eligible and randomized to one of the three trial arms”
_evidence.method_summary: Eligible households were randomized to one of three trial arms, but the specific sequence-generation method is not described.
context_clues: randomized to one of the three trial arms

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article states that eligible households were “randomized to one of the three trial arms,” but it does not describe any mechanism for concealing the upcoming allocation from the person enrolling participants. Comment: Because allocation concealment requires explicit evidence of a preventing mechanism (e.g., central allocation or SNOSE) and none is reported, the risk of bias is Unclear rather than Low or High.

_evidence.note: The article describes that households were 'randomized to one of the three trial arms,' but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was concealed from recruiters/allocators.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article does not state whether participants or personnel were blinded, nor does it describe any blinding method. It only reports that this was a community-randomized controlled trial and describes the cash transfer interventions and outcomes (including subjective outcomes such as child chronic illness/disability and school attendance). Because blinding status is unspecified, there is insufficient evidence to judge low or high risk for performance bias.

Summary: The outcome measures were mixed, including subjective/judgement-dependent outcomes and objective outcomes. The article states: "Outcome measures included child chronic illness and disability, birth registration status, and school attendance." Chronic illness and disability were defined by reported symptoms/impairment (e.g. "very sick for at least 3 months"; "an impairment that seriously affects ability to perform routine work, school and/or other daily functions"), while birth registration and school attendance were also reported. Comment: Because the study includes subjective outcomes that depend on reported assessment and the paper does not describe blinded outcome assessment, knowledge of allocation could have influenced measurement of at least the subjective outcomes. Under the worst-outcome rule, this makes the domain high risk even though some outcomes are objective.

Quote: "Of the 11,820 households completing a baseline census questionnaire... 4,043 were determined to be eligible and randomized to one of the three trial arms, and 3,818 (94 %) of these were followed up 1.5 years later." Comment: The analysed follow-up sample was 94% of the randomized households overall, indicating low total attrition at the study time point. Although the paper notes some differential attrition by child/household characteristics, the reported follow-up rate is high and there is no evidence here of large or clearly outcome-related post-randomization loss on the analysed outcomes.

_evidence.missing_data_method: mixed-effects models; no explicit attrition handling method reported
_evidence.notes: Analysed sample numbers are reported for the school-aged child subset (N=5,331) within 1,697 households; overall follow-up was 3,818/4,043 households (94%), implying 225/4,043 lost to follow-up (5.6%). The excerpt also notes 31 deceased, 396 lost/new to study, and 514 left household in the child-level subset, but these are not arm-specific.
